title: Before China
bullets: Western China drops east in three giant steps from the Tibetan Plateau through mid-altitude mountains to the great plains — and everything in this book happens on the bottom two.
The Yellow River carries so much loess silt it is literally called China's Sorrow, changing course at least 26 times in recorded history and drowning whole regions when it jumps its banks.
The Yangtze is the opposite kind of river — longer, wetter, slower, subtropical — and its warm standing water made wet-rice agriculture possible.
Ancient China had two cradles, not one: millet in the dry loess north, rice in the wet southern delta, and their separate traditions would shape Chinese culture for eight thousand years.
Loess soil across northern China is so fine and fertile that a person with a wooden digging stick can plant a crop in it — one of the reasons farming took root there so easily.
More major ancient Chinese sites come from Henan province (the elbow where the Yellow River turns east) than any other province on earth.
Around 7000 BCE, hunter-gatherers in two river valleys 600 miles apart stopped just gathering wild grain and started planting it — the moment China was no longer just a landscape.

title: Two Grains, Two Worlds
bullets: The Peiligang culture of the middle Yellow River was already farming foxtail and broomcorn millet, keeping pigs and dogs, and living in semi-subterranean pit houses by 7000 BCE.

At Cishan in the Taihang foothills, archaeologists found dozens of underground storage pits holding tens of thousands of kilograms of carbonized millet — the earliest large-scale grain storage anywhere in the world.
In the warm marshes of the Yangtze, communities at Kuahuqiao and Shangshan domesticated rice around 7000 BCE — the crop that would eventually feed more than half of humanity.

At Jiahu in central Henan, archaeologists found the oldest playable musical instruments on earth: bone flutes made from red-crowned crane wing bones, drilled to produce a recognizable musical scale 9,000 years ago.
The Jiahu cemetery also produced carved tortoise shells with at least 11 distinct incised symbols — possibly the earliest Chinese proto-writing, thousands of years before any confirmed script.
The north-south grain split — millet in the dry loess, rice paddies in the subtropical south — would define Chinese agriculture and culture for the next eight millennia.

title: The Painted Pottery World
bullets: The Yangshao culture of the Yellow River valley produced vividly painted red-and-black pottery covered in spirals, fish, deer, and the iconic human-face-and-fish motif — an artistic grammar that would echo on Shang bronzes 2,000 years later.
Banpo village near modern Xi'an was a 5-hectare moated settlement of pit houses, pottery kilns, and burial grounds, continuously occupied from about 4800 to 3750 BCE.
Over a hundred distinct incised symbols on Yangshao pottery rims — the Banpo pottery marks — form one of the earliest systematic symbol systems in China.
The Hemudu culture of the Yangtze delta built longhouses on wooden stilts over tidal flats, used mortise-and-tenon joinery, and produced the earliest known Chinese lacquerwork.

Traces of silk fiber found at late Yangshao and Liangzhu sites date to around 3500 BCE — Chinese silk production began thousands of years before anywhere else in the world, pairing with Indus cotton as the two signature Asian fibers.

Chinese tradition remembered this era through the legendary sage-kings Fuxi (inventor of the bagua trigrams and ancestor of the I Ching) and Shennong, the Divine Farmer who tasted hundreds of plants to test their properties.

title: Jade and the Pig-Dragon
bullets: Nephrite jade is so tough it is almost impossible to shatter, and before metal tools it could only be shaped by grinding it down with abrasive sand — a process that took weeks or months per object.
The Hongshan culture of northeastern China built the earliest known Chinese ritual landscape at Niuheliang, with stone-circle altars, a goddess temple, and hilltop platforms, around 3500 BCE.
Hongshan jade workers carved the zhulong, or pig-dragon — C-shaped coiled creatures with pig-like snouts and serpentine bodies — the earliest dragon images in Chinese art.
That same coiled dragon form would persist almost unchanged on Shang bronzes, Han jade, and Ming imperial robes for five thousand years.
Hongshan elite burials show the first clear evidence of ancestor worship: rich graves with ritual vessels used to feed and honor the dead.
Jade became the material of cosmic power — the substance the living used to talk to the dead — a tradition that would persist unbroken through every Chinese dynasty.

title: The Liangzhu Miracle
bullets: Liangzhu in the Yangtze delta was a 300-hectare walled city with a central palace precinct on a raised earthen platform — a bit smaller than New York's Central Park, and a thousand years older than the Xia Dynasty.
An integrated hydraulic system of dams, dikes, canals, and reservoirs covered more than 100 square kilometers — the largest water-control project anywhere in the world for another 2,000 years.

Liangzhu produced vast quantities of jade bi discs (round, symbolizing heaven) and cong tubes (square-sectioned, symbolizing earth) — encoding the Chinese round-sky-over-square-earth cosmology millennia before it was written down.
Elite Liangzhu burials contained hundreds of jade objects per person, and over 600 Liangzhu artifacts bear systematic incised proto-symbols — the most complex pre-dynastic marking system in China.

Around 2300 BCE Liangzhu collapsed suddenly, probably from catastrophic flooding — possibly on the leading edge of the 4.2 kiloyear event, the same global drought that contributed to the fall of Akkad and the decline of the Indus Valley.
The Chinese Great Flood legend may preserve a distant oral memory of the Liangzhu catastrophe, compressed into the Yu the Great story thousands of years later.

title: Longshan — Walls, Warlords, and the Yellow Emperor
bullets: Longshan potters mastered fast kick-wheels and reduction firing to produce eggshell-thin jet-black ritual cups with walls well under a millimeter thick — the pinnacle of Neolithic ceramic technology.
Rammed-earth (hangtu) walls 16 to 50 feet thick at the base enclosed walled towns of up to 400 hectares, using the same monumental construction technique that would build every later Chinese capital.

At Shimao in Shaanxi, builders buried pits of sacrificed young women under the city walls as foundation offerings around 2000 BCE — a direct parallel to the contemporary Royal Tombs of Ur, while the Indus cities did nothing of the kind.

Chinese tradition remembers the era through the Yellow Emperor (Huangdi), who supposedly unified the tribes of the Yellow River at the Battle of Zhuolu against the warrior Chi You with his iron head and bronze forehead.

Legend credits the Yellow Emperor's minister Cangjie with inventing Chinese writing by studying the tracks of birds and animals — when he finished, heaven rained down millet and the ghosts of the dead wept through the night.

Longshan diviners began practicing scapulimancy — heating cattle shoulder blades until they cracked and reading the patterns — the direct unwritten ancestor of the Shang oracle bones that would appear a thousand years later.
The Taosi observatory in Shanxi is the oldest purpose-built solar observatory yet identified anywhere in the world, with 13 rammed-earth pillars aligned to solstices and equinoxes around 2300 BCE.

title: The Flood and the First Dynasty
bullets: Chinese tradition tells of a Great Flood that drowned the world for generations until the legendary engineer Yu the Great spent 13 years dredging rivers — working so intensely he passed his own home three times without stopping to see his family.
Yu's reward was the throne, but when Yu died his son Qi seized power, ending the merit-based succession of the sage-kings and founding the Xia Dynasty, the first hereditary Chinese dynasty.

In 2016, a Science paper identified physical evidence for a catastrophic outburst flood at Jishi Gorge on the upper Yellow River around 1920 BCE — hundreds of times the river's normal flow, squarely inside the 4.2 kiloyear climate window.
The Yu flood turns out to be the Chinese echo of the same global climate disruption that ended Akkad, shook the Indus, and disrupted Egypt — four Bronze Age civilizations, one shared catastrophe.

The Xia Zheng calendar, attributed to the Xia Dynasty, sets the first month of the year at the new moon after the winter solstice — the basis for the Chinese lunisolar calendar and the Chinese New Year still observed by over a billion people today.

The Xia tradition established the template of dynastic succession and the Mandate of Heaven — the idea that rulers must earn their right to rule and can be legitimately overthrown when they lose it, a pattern that would repeat for four thousand years.

title: Erlitou — Legend Becomes Archaeology
bullets: The Erlitou site near Luoyang, found by accident in 1959, is the strongest physical candidate for the legendary Xia Dynasty capital — a 1.2-square-mile settlement with palace architecture, bronze workshops, and long-distance trade.

Erlitou's Palace No. 1 sat on a 355-by-330-foot rammed-earth platform with a central hall, surrounding colonnade, and south-facing entrance — the template for every Chinese imperial capital from Shang Anyang to the Ming Forbidden City.

Erlitou bronze workers used piece-mold casting to produce the first true bronze ritual vessels in China — a technique unique to China, while Mesopotamia and the Indus used lost-wax casting on objects like the Dancing Girl.
Chinese bronze casting arrived around 1,500 years later than Mesopotamian bronze and 700 years after the Indus, but it was immediately embedded in the ritual and political culture of an early state.

Erlitou craftsmen inlaid bronze plaques with hundreds of tiny turquoise chips imported from over 600 miles away, creating the earliest Chinese turquoise-mosaic animal masks — the ancestors of the Shang taotie.

Chinese tradition says the last Xia king Jie was a tyrant who built a pool of wine and a forest of meat while the peasants starved, and when Tang of Shang defeated him at the Battle of Mingtiao around 1600 BCE, the Mandate of Heaven passed to the Shang Dynasty.

Everything later Chinese civilization would consider essential — rice and millet agriculture, jade ritual, dragons, ancestors, walls, kings, bronze, and the Mandate of Heaven — was already in place by 1600 BCE. The Shang inherited it all.
